title: Deep scratches remain after 80 grit
description: 80 grit removed material but left deep scratches.
customer_phrases: Deep scratches remain after 80 grit
80 grit removed material but left deep scratches.
Clean the surface.
Sand evenly with 120 grit until the 80-grit marks are lighter.
aliases: 80 grit is a heavy removal grit and leaves a scratch pattern that must be refined in steps.
120, then 180, then 220 or 320 as needed.
Do not jump from 80 directly to 320 or finer.

title: 320 grit does not remove 180 grit scratches
description: Scratches from 180 grit remain after sanding with 320 grit.
customer_phrases: 320 grit does not remove 180 grit scratches
Scratches from 180 grit remain after sanding with 320 grit.
Return to 220 or 240 grit.
Use light, even pressure.
aliases: The jump from 180 to 320 is too large for the depth of the scratches.
220 or 240 before returning to 320.
Do not press harder with 320 grit to force removal.

title: Coarse scratches show on wood
description: Wood still shows visible scratch lines after prep sanding.
customer_phrases: Coarse scratches show on wood
Wood still shows visible scratch lines after prep sanding.
Sand with the grain when possible.
Use the grit that still removes the visible lines.
aliases: The previous grit pattern was not fully removed before moving finer.
120 to remove heavy marks, then 180 and 220 for prep.
Do not skip directly to fine finishing grits on rough wood.

title: Scratches remain on metal after prep
description: Metal shows straight or uneven sanding scratches.
customer_phrases: Scratches remain on metal after prep
Metal shows straight or uneven sanding scratches.
Remove residue from the metal.
Sand with the next finer grit until the old pattern is uniform.
aliases: The metal was not refined through enough intermediate grits.
180, 220, 320, then 400 or 600 if a finer prep is needed.
Do not leave loose grit or debris on the surface.

title: Deep lines remain on clear coat
description: Fine sanding leaves some deeper lines in clear coat.
customer_phrases: Deep lines remain on clear coat
Fine sanding leaves some deeper lines in clear coat.
Rinse the surface and paper.
Use clean water and light pressure.
aliases: A previous grit or debris created scratches deeper than the current fine grit can remove.
Return one step coarser only as needed, then progress 1000, 1500, 2000, 3000.
Do not dry sand a fine clear coat finish unless the coating instructions call for it.

title: Paint clogs the sheet quickly
description: Paint residue loads into the abrasive and sanding slows.
customer_phrases: Paint clogs the sheet quickly
Paint residue loads into the abrasive and sanding slows.
Clean the painted surface.
Use lighter pressure.
aliases: Soft paint or coating residue is smearing under pressure.
Use the grit needed for the stage; often 120 to 220 for removal or prep.
Do not keep sanding with a glazed or loaded sheet.

title: Primer dust loads the paper
description: Primer dust packs into the abrasive during prep.
customer_phrases: Primer dust loads the paper
Primer dust packs into the abrasive during prep.
Let the primer cure as directed by the coating maker.
Use light pressure.
aliases: Dust is not being cleared often enough or the sheet is worn.
320 to 600 depending on the coating stage.
Do not sand uncured coating.

title: Wood dust clogs the paper
description: Wood dust fills the abrasive and the sheet feels smooth.
customer_phrases: Wood dust clogs the paper
Wood dust fills the abrasive and the sheet feels smooth.
Brush or wipe dust away often.
Use lighter pressure.
aliases: Dust buildup, excess pressure, or a worn sheet is reducing cutting.
Use the correct prep grit, commonly 120 to 220 for wood smoothing.
Do not use a clogged sheet for final prep.

title: Finish smears while sanding
description: The finish gums up instead of sanding to powder.
customer_phrases: Finish smears while sanding
The finish gums up instead of sanding to powder.
Stop and clean the sheet.
Allow more cure time if the coating is soft.
aliases: The finish may be too soft for sanding or too much heat is being created.
Use fine prep grits such as 320 to 600 when the finish is ready.
Do not grind soft finish with heavy pressure.

title: Wet sanding slurry builds up
description: Wet sanding residue collects and makes cutting inconsistent.
customer_phrases: Wet sanding slurry builds up
Wet sanding residue collects and makes cutting inconsistent.
Keep clean water on the surface.
Rinse the sheet often.
aliases: The surface and sheet are not being rinsed often enough.
Use the planned fine grit sequence, such as 1000 to 3000.
Do not let dirty slurry dry on the work area.

title: Wood still rough after 120 grit
description: Wood fibers or roughness remain after sanding with 120 grit.
customer_phrases: Wood still rough after 120 grit
Wood fibers or roughness remain after sanding with 120 grit.
Sand evenly with 150 or 180.
Clean dust.
aliases: 120 grit may still be an early prep step and needs follow-up refinement.
150 or 180, then 220 for smoother prep.
Do not jump from rough 120 grit directly to very fine grit.

title: Wood still rough after 220 grit
description: The wood still feels raised or uneven after 220 grit.
customer_phrases: Wood still rough after 220 grit
The wood still feels raised or uneven after 220 grit.
Identify whether roughness is across the whole surface or only spots.
Return to 180 on rough areas.
aliases: The roughness was not fully removed at the earlier grit or the grain was raised.
Return to 180 if roughness remains, then finish with 220.
Do not polish rough wood with 320 before it is leveled.

title: Metal still feels rough
description: Metal still has roughness after sanding.
customer_phrases: Metal still feels rough
Metal still has roughness after sanding.
Remove loose debris.
Sand with a grit that cuts the roughness.
aliases: Burrs, oxidation, or deeper scratches need a grit that still cuts them.
Start around 120 or 180 for roughness, then 220 and 320.
Do not start too fine on rough metal.

title: Plastic still feels rough
description: Plastic remains rough or uneven after sanding.
customer_phrases: Plastic still feels rough
Plastic remains rough or uneven after sanding.
Clean the plastic.
Use light pressure.
aliases: The surface needs gentler step-by-step refinement to avoid deep scratches.
320 or 400 for prep, then 600+ for finer finish needs.
Do not use heavy pressure that can gouge or heat the plastic.

title: Rough spots remain after fine grit
description: Most of the surface is smooth but some spots remain rough.
customer_phrases: Rough spots remain after fine grit
Most of the surface is smooth but some spots remain rough.
Mark the rough spots visually if needed.
Sand those areas evenly with the working grit.
aliases: The rough spots were not leveled before moving to a finer grit.
Return to the last grit that can remove the roughness.
Do not overwork one tiny spot and create a low area.

title: Normal haze after wet sanding
description: Wet sanding leaves the surface dull or cloudy.
customer_phrases: Normal haze after wet sanding
Wet sanding leaves the surface dull or cloudy.
Keep the surface wet.
Use light pressure.
aliases: Fine wet sanding normally creates a uniform haze before polishing or final refinement.
Do not expect sanding alone to restore gloss.

title: Clear coat haze after 1000 grit
description: 1000 grit wet sanding leaves a dull clear coat.
customer_phrases: Clear coat haze after 1000 grit
1000 grit wet sanding leaves a dull clear coat.
Rinse the area.
Refine with 1500 grit.
aliases: 1000 grit is often an intermediate leveling step, not the final gloss stage.
1500, then 2000 and 3000 before polishing.
Do not stop at 1000 grit when a gloss finish is expected.

title: Plastic haze after wet sanding
description: Plastic looks cloudy after fine wet sanding.
customer_phrases: Plastic haze after wet sanding
Plastic looks cloudy after fine wet sanding.
Use clean water.
Use very light pressure.
aliases: The surface has been refined but still needs finer grits or polishing if gloss is desired.
1500, 2000, and 3000 for polishing preparation.
Do not create heat with heavy pressure.

title: Wet sanding haze is uneven
description: Some areas are dull while others are still glossy or scratched.
customer_phrases: Wet sanding haze is uneven
Some areas are dull while others are still glossy or scratched.
Rinse the surface.
Sand with overlapping strokes.
aliases: Pressure, water, or sanding coverage was inconsistent.
Repeat the current fine grit before moving finer.
Do not polish an unevenly sanded surface and expect uniform results.

title: Skipped from 120 to 320
description: 320 grit does not remove the scratches left by 120 grit.
customer_phrases: Skipped from 120 to 320
320 grit does not remove the scratches left by 120 grit.
Return to 180 or 220.
Sand until the 120-grit lines are gone.
aliases: The grit progression skipped too many intermediate steps.
Use 180 or 220 before 320.
Do not use fine grit as a substitute for proper progression.

title: Choosing a starting grit
description: It is unclear which grit to start with.
customer_phrases: Choosing a starting grit
It is unclear which grit to start with.
Identify whether you are removing, smoothing, finishing, or polishing prep.
Start with the least aggressive grit that works.
aliases: The task stage has not been identified yet.
60-120 for removal, 150-240 for prep, 280-400 for finish prep, 600+ for fine stages.
Do not start with the coarsest grit unless heavy removal is needed.

title: Using the 60-3000 assorted kit
description: The kit has many grits and the right order is unclear.
customer_phrases: Using the 60-3000 assorted kit
The kit has many grits and the right order is unclear.
Choose the grit range for the task stage.
Do not use every grit if the surface does not need it.
aliases: Each grit range serves a different stage.
Use 80-120 for removal, 180-400 for prep, 600-3000 for fine finishing and polishing prep.
Do not use 1000-3000 for heavy removal.

title: What grit for wood prep
description: The wood needs to be prepared but the grit is unclear.
customer_phrases: What grit for wood prep
The wood needs to be prepared but the grit is unclear.
Start only as coarse as needed.
Sand evenly with the grain when possible.
aliases: Wood prep depends on how rough the surface is before sanding.
120 or 150 for rough prep, then 180 or 220 for smoother prep.
Do not over-sand soft areas with heavy pressure.

title: What grit for paint prep
description: Paint needs prep sanding before another coat.
customer_phrases: What grit for paint prep
Paint needs prep sanding before another coat.
Let the coating cure as directed.
Use light pressure.
aliases: Paint prep needs enough tooth without deep scratches.
220 to 400 depending on the stage and coating instructions.
Do not use heavy-removal grit between finish coats.

title: Grit is too coarse between coats
description: Sanding between coats leaves scratches or cuts through coating.
customer_phrases: Grit is too coarse between coats
Sanding between coats leaves scratches or cuts through coating.
Confirm the coating is ready to sand.
Use light pressure.
aliases: The grit is intended for removal instead of light intercoat prep.
320 to 600 depending on coating stage.
Do not use 80, 120, or 180 grit for fine intercoat sanding.

title: Sanding cuts through between coats
description: Sanding removes too much coating in some areas.
customer_phrases: Sanding cuts through between coats
Sanding removes too much coating in some areas.
Reduce pressure.
Use a finer grit.
aliases: Pressure is too high, grit is too coarse, or edges are being overworked.
Use a finer grit such as 400 or 600 for delicate intercoat smoothing.
Do not try to level thick defects with fine intercoat sanding.

title: Primer scratches show through
description: Scratches remain visible after coating over primer.
customer_phrases: Primer scratches show through
Scratches remain visible after coating over primer.
Sand the primer evenly.
Remove coarse scratch marks.
aliases: The primer was not refined enough before the next coat.
320 or 400 before recoating, depending on coating instructions.
Do not coat over isolated deep sanding marks.

title: Swirls remain after 1000 grit
description: Curved or circular marks remain after fine sanding.
customer_phrases: Swirls remain after 1000 grit
Curved or circular marks remain after fine sanding.
Clean the area.
Use light pressure.
aliases: 1000 grit marks may need further refinement or the pattern was inconsistent.
1500, 2000, then 3000 as needed.
Do not press harder with the same grit to chase swirls.

title: Swirls from dirty wet sanding water
description: Fine sanding creates random curved scratches.
customer_phrases: Swirls from dirty wet sanding water
Fine sanding creates random curved scratches.
Rinse the surface.
Use clean water.
aliases: Loose grit or debris in the water is scratching the surface.
Repeat the current fine grit, then move finer.
Do not keep sanding with contaminated slurry.

title: Random swirl pattern remains
description: A visible pattern remains after sanding.
customer_phrases: Random swirl pattern remains
A visible pattern remains after sanding.
Identify the deepest visible marks.
Return one grit step if needed.
aliases: The final grit was not fine enough or the previous scratches were not fully removed.
Move gradually through finer grits up to the finish goal.
Do not skip refinement steps when the finish will be inspected closely.

title: Paper tears on sharp edges
description: The sheet catches and rips on an edge or corner.
customer_phrases: Paper tears on sharp edges
The sheet catches and rips on an edge or corner.
Inspect the edge.
Use lighter pressure.
aliases: Sharp edges cut into the backing under pressure.
Use the correct task grit, but reduce pressure near edges.
Do not pull the sheet hard across sharp corners.

title: Paper tears at the fold
description: The sheet splits where it was folded.
customer_phrases: Paper tears at the fold
The sheet splits where it was folded.
Fold gently without crushing the backing.
Use a smaller piece if needed.
aliases: A hard crease concentrates stress in one line.
Any grit can tear if sharply creased.
Do not create a tight crease and pull hard under pressure.

title: Paper tears from heavy pressure
description: The sheet wears through or tears during sanding.
customer_phrases: Paper tears from heavy pressure
The sheet wears through or tears during sanding.
Reduce pressure.
Let the abrasive do the cutting.
aliases: Excess pressure creates heat and stress on the backing.
Use a grit coarse enough to cut without forcing it.
Do not use pressure to make a too-fine grit remove material.

title: Paint removal is too slow
description: Paint is not coming off efficiently.
customer_phrases: Paint removal is too slow
Paint is not coming off efficiently.
Use a coarser grit suited to removal.
Clear dust and paint residue often.
aliases: The grit may be too fine, loaded, or worn for removal.
60, 80, or 120 for removal depending on the surface and finish goal.
Do not remove paint with 400+ grit unless only light scuffing is needed.

title: Fine grit used for removal
description: Sanding takes a long time and little material is removed.
customer_phrases: Fine grit used for removal
Sanding takes a long time and little material is removed.
Switch to a grit that actually cuts the defect.
Sand evenly.
aliases: Fine grit refines scratches but is not efficient for heavy removal.
60 to 120 for heavy removal, then step finer.
Do not keep rubbing with a fine grit when the defect is still high or deep.

title: Worn sheet is not cutting
description: The sheet feels smooth and sanding no longer changes the surface.
customer_phrases: Worn sheet is not cutting
The sheet feels smooth and sanding no longer changes the surface.
Inspect the abrasive surface.
Clear debris if lightly loaded.
aliases: The abrasive is worn or loaded.
Stay with the selected grit but use a fresh sheet.
Do not compensate for a worn sheet with heavy pressure.

title: Wood prep before finish
description: Wood needs a smooth, consistent surface before finish.
customer_phrases: Wood prep before finish
Wood needs a smooth, consistent surface before finish.
Start with the least aggressive grit that removes roughness.
Sand evenly.
aliases: The prep sequence has not reached an even final scratch pattern.
Do not leave coarse scratch marks under finish.

title: Metal prep before paint
description: Metal needs sanding before coating.
customer_phrases: Metal prep before paint
Metal needs sanding before coating.
Clean the metal.
Remove roughness or oxidation with the needed grit.
aliases: The surface needs cleaning and a consistent prep pattern.
180 to 320 depending on existing roughness and coating instructions.
Do not coat over loose debris or deep random scratches.

title: Plastic prep before paint
description: Plastic needs light sanding before coating.
customer_phrases: Plastic prep before paint
Plastic needs light sanding before coating.
Clean the plastic.
Use light pressure.
aliases: Plastic can scratch or heat easily if the grit or pressure is too aggressive.
320 to 600 depending on coating instructions and surface condition.
Do not gouge plastic with coarse grit unless heavy shaping is intentional.

title: Clear coat leveling before polish
description: Clear coat needs fine leveling before polishing.
customer_phrases: Clear coat leveling before polish
Clear coat needs fine leveling before polishing.
Confirm the coating is ready for sanding.
Use clean water and light pressure.
aliases: The surface has texture that requires controlled fine wet sanding.
1000 or 1500 to start only when appropriate, then 2000 and 3000.
Do not sand through edges or raised body lines.

title: Using the assorted kit for heavy removal
description: The task needs fast material removal.
customer_phrases: Using the assorted kit for heavy removal
The task needs fast material removal.
Start with 80 or 120 only if heavy removal is needed.
Stop once the defect is reduced.
aliases: Starting too fine makes sanding slow.
80 or 120, then refine with 180, 220, and 320 as needed.
Do not leave 80-grit scratches as the final surface.

title: Using the assorted kit for finish prep
description: The surface needs fine prep before coating or finishing.
customer_phrases: Using the assorted kit for finish prep
The surface needs fine prep before coating or finishing.
Use 220 or 320 to refine prep scratches.
Use 400 or 600 for lighter fine prep where appropriate.
aliases: Finish prep needs finer grits than heavy removal.
220, 320, 400, or 600 depending on finish stage.
Do not use 80 or 120 for fine finish prep.

title: Using the assorted kit for polishing prep
description: A glossy surface needs fine sanding before polish.
customer_phrases: Using the assorted kit for polishing prep
A glossy surface needs fine sanding before polish.
Start only as coarse as the defect requires.
Keep the surface wet.
aliases: The surface must be refined through high grits before polishing.
Do not use low grits for polishing prep.

title: Uneven finish from pressure
description: The sanded surface looks patchy or uneven.
customer_phrases: Uneven finish from pressure
The sanded surface looks patchy or uneven.
Sand with overlapping strokes.
Use even pressure.
aliases: Pressure or stroke coverage was inconsistent.
Repeat the current stage grit before moving finer.
Do not focus pressure in one small area.

title: Finger grooves from hand sanding
description: The surface has uneven grooves from fingers.
customer_phrases: Finger grooves from hand sanding
The surface has uneven grooves from fingers.
Use a sanding block on flat surfaces.
Keep pressure even.
aliases: Finger pressure concentrated force in narrow lines.
Use the current prep grit, then refine normally.
Do not press with fingertips on flat finish areas.

title: Patchy surface after grit change
description: The surface looks uneven after switching grits.
customer_phrases: Patchy surface after grit change
The surface looks uneven after switching grits.
Inspect the entire area.
Sand until the older pattern is gone.
aliases: The previous grit pattern was not fully removed across the whole surface.
Return to the previous grit or current grit until uniform.
Do not move finer while old scratches remain in spots.

title: Dust creates extra scratches
description: Random scratches appear while dry sanding.
customer_phrases: Dust creates extra scratches
Random scratches appear while dry sanding.
Stop and wipe the surface.
Clean or replace the sheet.
aliases: Loose grit or debris is trapped under the paper.
Repeat the current grit after cleaning, then move finer.
Do not sand over loose debris.

title: Grit comes off the sheet
description: Loose abrasive appears on the surface or the sheet sheds grit during sanding.
customer_phrases: Grit comes off the sheet
Loose abrasive appears on the surface or the sheet sheds grit during sanding.
Clean the surface and the sheet.
Use clean water and light pressure.
aliases: The sheet may be worn, creased, over-soaked, or used with heavy pressure on a rough edge.
Use a fresh sheet in the same grit, or step coarser only if the task is too slow.
Do not let dirty slurry or trapped grit stay under the sheet.

title: Sheet curls during wet sanding
description: The sheet curls up and becomes hard to keep flat while wet sanding.
customer_phrases: Sheet curls during wet sanding
The sheet curls up and becomes hard to keep flat while wet sanding.
Clean the surface and the sheet.
Use clean water and light pressure.
aliases: Water exposure and hand pressure can make a cut piece curl, especially if it is not supported evenly.
Use the planned wet sanding grit, commonly 800 to 3000 for fine stages.
Do not expect sanding alone to create full gloss.

title: Sheet becomes soft after soaking too long
description: The sheet feels weak or soft after sitting in water.

Clean the surface and the sheet.
Use clean water and light pressure.
aliases: Extended soaking can soften the backing and make handling less controlled.
Use the intended wet sanding grit with a fresh piece.
Do not let dirty slurry or trapped grit stay under the sheet.

title: Sheet edge frays during sanding

customer_phrases: Sheet edge frays during sanding
The edge of the sandpaper frays and leaves fibers or rough backing.
Clean the surface and the sheet.
Use clean water and light pressure.
aliases: The sheet edge is rubbing against corners, burrs, or a sharp surface under pressure.
Use the same working grit, with lighter pressure near edges.
Do not let dirty slurry or trapped grit stay under the sheet.

title: Sandpaper leaves residue
description: Residue from dust, slurry, or loaded coating remains after sanding.

Clean the surface and the sheet.
Use clean water and light pressure.
aliases: Loaded abrasive, dirty wet sanding water, or loose sanding dust is being spread over the surface.
Stay with the current grit, then move finer only after cleaning.
Do not let dirty slurry or trapped grit stay under the sheet.

title: Sheet feels smooth but looks unused

The sheet does not look worn out, but it feels smooth and stops cutting.
Clean the surface and the sheet.
Use clean water and light pressure.
aliases: Fine dust or finish residue can glaze the abrasive surface before the sheet looks fully used.
Use a fresh sheet in the same grit, or choose a grit that better matches the task.
Do not let dirty slurry or trapped grit stay under the sheet.

title: Sheet is hard to hold for detail sanding

Clean the surface.
Use the recommended grit with light, even pressure.
